text: So, of course, doing advanced surgery, many HPV surgery and liver surgery, you have to deal with bleeding. I think the liver is a bleeding organ. But you have to learn how to overcome some of the situation.

I'm teaching that one of the trick of dealing with bleeding is try to superficialize the section line of the liver in order to have it always under control. And if you need to stitch and to put needles, even large needles and suture, you are comfortable in doing that.
Working in coordination with your anesthesiology, usually majority of bleeding are easily controlled. But there are situations where the bleeding might require other kind of tricks.

The surgeon has completely exposed the vena cava during a left hepatectomy extended to a portion of segment eight. A selective suture is placed on the vena cava, effectively controlling the bleeding.

The surgeon completes the transaction of the left lobe, revealing the vena cava underneath. Two ducts from the right system are visible, and a long jejunal loop is brought up to the liver for a Roux-en-Y reconstruction following the extended hepatectomy.

So I think the message here, different kind of bleeding can happen during liver surgery and the majority of those bleeding can be controlled just being able to suture fast and very effectively. There are other situations where you might overcome with some tricks, like making a compression, speeding up the transaction, and having a better view.
